coredata.dc:description: The integration of high-energy lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) as energy storage solutions is increasing in the building sector (e.g., residential, schools, offices, commercial and industrial buildings). This increases the fire load, raising fire safety concerns, including characteristic fire behaviour, increased fire intensity, faster fire growth, prolonged burning duration, fire spread and flashover risk. Full-scale fire experiments with NMC battery modules of nominal 6600 Wh were conducted in an ISO 9705 compartment. The emphasis of the work was to analyse the fire characteristics within the room from the beginning of thermal runaway in a set of individual cells and continuous cell-to-cell thermal propagation in a single module. The results revealed that simultaneous cells in thermal runaway within a module intensified heat release into the room. The fire development differed from fires in common technical rooms, exhibiting flash fire, ultra-fast fire growth, intense thermal feedback and multiple heat release rate peaks. The experimental findings provide critical data to support risk assessments of battery energy storage systems. Currently, the lack of guidelines on fire safety requirements for battery rooms limits the ability to design rooms with appropriate active and passive fire safety measures. Future research should further explore fire spread between modules and the effectiveness of active and passive fire protection measures in confined compartments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Side, top and front views, including the battery module, the structural elements and the instrumentation within the ISO 9705 room. The locations of the objects are referenced to the origin of coordinates, as indicated in the top view. All units are given in meters. PTC, TC and TC_Tree refer to the plate thermometers, single thermocouples and thermocouples distributed in trees, respectively.

Experimental set up and instrumentation within the ISO 9705 room. The illustration shows the position of the battery module upper three lightweight solid concrete blocks, structural concrete elements, pilot flame, plate thermocouple (PTCs), thermocouples (TCs), and Gopro cameras. PTC_WL, PTC_WH, PTC_J, PTC_C and PTC_M refer to the plate thermometers for the wall low, wall high, jet, ceiling and module, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1. The distribution of thermocouples is indicated for TC_TreeJet.

Top view figure: Configuration of the battery module: battery cells (1 to 28), plastic holders, plastic cover with ventilation opening, plastic endplate and metal casing. TC in the different cells are indicated with red dots. Side view figure: The distances A and B of TCs from the cell edges are indicated by a red dot in cell number 28 and in cell number 1. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The horizontal axis shows the cell number, while the vertical axis represents the time elapsed (min) since the occurrence of the first thermal runaway for all the experiments. The vertical axis at 0 indicates how many individual cells contributed to the initial thermal runaway. The figure is based in Meraner et al. [40].

Cell surface temperature vs time (min) within the battery modules during all the experiments. Thermocouples were placed within the battery module (TC_batt1 to TC_batt28) as described in section 2.3. Each image shows selected thermocouples located on cells at both the edges and the centre of the battery. Some TCs were not included because the measurements after thermal runaway were affected by noise.
Images of the fire development for Experiment 1.
Images of the jet flame directions from Experiment 1 captured by GoPro cameras: The observed jet flame directions include horizontal and diffusive flames with two inclination angles, 60 and 90°.
Images of the fire development for Experiment 2.

Gas temperatures measured in the three thermocouple trees a) TreeJet (at 230 cm, 130 cm and 80 cm), b) TreeMiddle (at 230 cm, 130 cm and 80 cm), and c) TreeBack (at 230 cm, 130 cm and 80 cm), ISO 834 fire curve and hydrocarbon fire curve are represented during experiment 1, 2, and 3. The temperature range for the three thermocouples trees, from 230 cm to 80 cm, is illustrated as a coloured grey area for all experiments.

Incident radiation (kW/m2) is represented using two plate thermometers in all experiments: a) Plate thermometer is positioned in front of jet flame (PTC_WL), and b) Plate thermometer is oriented toward the ceiling (PTC_C).
Gas measurements of CO2 and CO in the exhaust duct of the ISO 9705 setup. Results from Experiment 3 are not available due to a malfunction in the gas analyser.

For Experiment 1: (a) The heat release rate (kW) was measured over a 70-min period. (b) The heat release rate (kW) of the LIB module was specifically examined between 19 and 29 min to capture the detailed combustion behaviour during this timeframe.

For Experiment 2: (a) The heat release rate (kW) was recorded over a duration of 70 min. (b) The heat release rate (kW) of the LIB module was specifically analysed from 19 min to 26 min to capture detailed combustion dynamics during this period.

Representation of heat release rate (kW) by OCC and CDG methods, and the ultra-fast fire curve with growth constant of 1.25 min/√MW. a) Heat release rate from 19 min to 22 min for Experiment 1, b) Heat release rate from 20 min to 23 min for Experiment 2.
Average heating rate until the first TR in the battery module estimated based on measured cell surface temperature of cell number 1 to 10.
Battery module description.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of fire development in Experiment 1, including the observation from video recordings, and corresponding figures.

entry.bold: Start of battery gas venting. The colour of the gas was grey. Observation of a purple burner flame.
Grey smoke started and continued to come out of the room door.
Gas venting was observed covering the battery module.
Large amounts of smoke were released. Sparks from the vent area of the battery ignited the gas.
Smoke gas layer was ignited, and a flash fire was observed.
Electrolyte and plastics were burning, and plastics were melting outside the module case.
Burner was shut off, and the layer of smoke began to dilute.
Start of the second horizontal jet flame to the end of the third diffusive jet flame. Following a decay of the jet intensity.
Start of the fourth horizontal jet flame to the end of the seventh diffusive jet flame.
Start of the eighth horizontal jet flame to the end of the ninth diffusive jet flame.
Start of the tenth horizontal jet flame to the end of the thirteenth diffusive jet flame.
Flame began to shorten, and the fire was abated.

Second to third horizontal jet flame and to third diffusive jet flame.
Fourth to seventh horizontal jet flame and to seventh diffusive jet flame.
Eighth to ninth horizontal jet flame and to ninth diffusive jet flame.
Tenth to thirteenth horizontal jet flame and to thirteenth diffusive jet flame.
Flame height began to shorten, and fire was abated.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The integration of high-energy lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) as energy storage solutions is increasing in the building sector (e.g., residential, schools, offices, commercial and industrial buildings). This increases the fire load, raising fire safety concerns, including characteristic fire behaviour, increased fire intensity, faster fire growth, prolonged burning duration, fire spread and flashover risk. Full-scale fire experiments with NMC battery modules of nominal 6600 Wh were conducted in an ISO 9705 compartment. The emphasis of the work was to analyse the fire characteristics within the room from the beginning of thermal runaway in a set of individual cells and continuous cell-to-cell thermal propagation in a single module. The results revealed that simultaneous cells in thermal runaway within a module intensified heat release into the room. The fire development differed from fires in common technical rooms, exhibiting flash fire, ultra-fast fire growth, intense thermal feedback and multiple heat release rate peaks. The experimental findings provide critical data to support risk assessments of battery energy storage systems. Currently, the lack of guidelines on fire safety requirements for battery rooms limits the ability to design rooms with appropriate active and passive fire safety measures. Future research should further explore fire spread between modules and the effectiveness of active and passive fire protection measures in confined compartments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Abbreviations

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the use of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) as battery energy storage systems (BESS) has grown in maritime and electrical utility sectors, serving as an effective solution for storing energy from intermittent renewable sources and providing backup during power outages. The technology is expected to increase significantly in the built environment from 2025 [1,2], with residential buildings potentially storing up to 20 kWh and commercial or industrial buildings ranging from 100 kWh to 1 MWh. Since 2015, some fire accidents have occurred in existing BESS (e.g. battery room fire within an outdoor enclosure [3]). The Electric Power Research Institute (EPRI) database on BESS failure incidents provides insights into the root causes and failed components across 26 documented cases. Most failures were attributed to issues related to system integration, assembly and construction, and operation. Among these, integration was identified as the most common root cause, involving components responsible for balancing the system. Failures due to component incompatibility, such as mismatched cabling, inadequate ventilation systems, and other integration-related issues, were observed. Operation was the second most prevalent root cause, with failures primarily occurring within the control systems (e.g., voltage limits and state-of-charge (SoC) management) [4,5]. Fire and/or explosion in BESS can result in severe consequences such as injury or loss of life, destruction of the installation, and structural damage or collapse of the building.

Cell thermal runaway (TR) is an uncontrollable self-heating process in battery cells [6,7], which can propagate from cell-to-cell, module to module, and even rack to rack, significantly impacting fire development in the room. Four stages are associated with the process of TR, for example, when cells are exposed to external heating (e.g. electrical heating, fire heating or radiant heating) [8–11]: cell pre-heating, heat generation or self-heating, exponential temperature increase (TR stage) and cooling down. TR leads to the emission of flammable and toxic gases (e.g., H2, CO2, CO and smaller amounts of CH4, C2H4 and C2H6), as well as HF (e.g., from decomposition of fluorinated electrolyte salts (LiPF6)), which represents a hazardous toxic species generated during TR events. The process is accompanied by a substantial release of thermal energy [8]. The cathode composition notably impacts the total heat release [12]; for example, Sturk et al. suggested that oxygen release from the NMC cathode material during thermal decomposition may contribute to self-sustained combustion, although it does not provide experimental evidence [13]. Cell chemistry, cell format/geometry, cell energy density, cell degradation over time and cell state of charge (SoC) are parameters that exert a degree of influence on the initiation of TR. The experimental conditions also influence the initiation of TR [14]. Cell safety mechanisms, such as a pressure relief valve, release off-gases after a certain pressure increase (e.g., from volatile electrolyte solvents) typically before the onset of the TR stage. A common criterion to identify the start of TR is a sudden voltage drop to zero and/or a rapid increase in cell surface temperature [15].

The fire dynamics of a LIB module may comprise multiple heat release peaks [16]. The hot gases released by the cells during TR may result in the overheating of neighbouring cells, thereby contributing to the propagation of TR. The ignition of combustible gases may result in the formation of jet flames, which may have the potential to spread rapidly and significantly elevate the temperature within the module [15]. How fast the fire will spread depends on the battery design, such as the configuration of thermal insulating materials [17] accommodated within the module case and the distance between cells [18]. The fire development of LIB modules during a TR event in enclosed spaces, such as technical rooms, remains insufficiently understood. LIBs include additional complexity compared to conventional solid fuels. The combustion characteristics of LIBs involve gas generation, possible delayed ignition, and multi-phase heat release, which challenge standard compartment fire behaviour assumptions. Key enclosure variables such as ventilation, room geometry, and fuel bed significantly affect the development of fires, yet current data do not adequately capture how LIBs behave in such environments. A key challenge remains the prevention of fire propagation within a single module, as it depends, in general, on the battery design. Active measures, not connected directly to the module, are in general expected to have little effect on the propagation within a module. Allowing fire to spread to adjacent modules is not considered an acceptable outcome in terms of fire safety design. According to the standard NFPA 855:2023 [19] and the Swedish guidelines [20], batteries have to be placed in a fire compartment to compensate for the increased fire risk. Hence, two questions arise, which are:
•
Is the standard ISO 834 time-temperature curve [21] suitable to evaluate the fire resistance against a LIB fire in a single module,
•
and how do the requirements change if the fire can spread to additional modules or even units?

To date, only a limited number of published studies have conducted full-scale and large-scale fire tests, primarily in large open areas, Ditch et al. [21], Blum et al. [22] and in ISO containers, Barowy et al. [23], which leaves these questions unanswered. The present study aims to address the first question and investigate the likelihood of the second scenario, i.e., module-to-module propagation, to occur.

Key sub-questions addressed in the present work include:
•
Is the fire growth of a LIB module rapid enough to qualify as an ultra-fast fire?
•
Does the thermal output from a single burning module pose an ignition risk to nearby materials and components?
•
Is the heat release rate (HRR) measured via oxygen consumption (OCC) and carbon dioxide generation (CDG) methods consistent?

This study contributes to the growing research on BESS fire safety and provides value for risk-informed design. It supports future fire testing strategies, fire modelling of battery room scenarios, and the development of more appropriate detection and extinguishing systems. The outcomes are particularly relevant for researchers studying battery combustion, fire engineers, BESS manufacturers, and those involved in risk assessment for buildings integrating distributed BESS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Trigger methods for full and large-scale li-ion battery fire tests
ce:section.ce:para: The trigger method refers to the method used to initiate a TR in at least one battery cell. The choice of trigger method is important in battery module fire experiments, as the number of cells undergoing TR can influence several critical fire parameters (e.g., gas release, heat release, smoke production, and mass loss). Previous research has studied the fire behaviour of LIB modules and units/racks (e.g. a unit or rack is a cabinet containing several interconnected modules) in full and large-scale tests. Fire experiments on LIB modules were performed in open areas [15,24–27], whereas fire experiments on LIB modules and units were in large open areas [22,28] and in ISO containers [23]. The objective of the studies of LIB modules was to gain insight into the processes of thermal combustion and TR propagation. Whereas the objective of LIB units in large open areas was to gain insight into the propagation of heat within different modules as a part of a battery unit, and the studies in ISO containers were to assess the propagation of heat and fire development. However, the studies in open areas do not look at the impact of fire on a room. Blum et al. used two ignition methods: a 400 kW propane burner (external ignition) and six 25 W cartridge heaters (internal ignition) [28]. Their findings indicated that the gas burner led to a more aggressive fire and larger damage, whereas only one module was affected in the case of using cartridge heaters. Ditch et al. used three flat bar heating elements of 900W to initiate ignition in the modules [22]. Their findings show that TR propagated cell-to-cell, and the ignition of one module affected all other modules within a rack. McKinnon and Barowy et al. used an inductive heating method (e.g. flexible film heater), increasing the temperature at a rate of 6 °C/min to ignite a mock-up cell [23,29]. Their findings suggest that the geometry and spacing within battery modules impact heat exposure to adjacent modules, and an increase in the gas temperature within the compartment can accelerate the TR propagation.

The aim of the study is to experimentally investigate the fire behaviour of NMC batteries in small rooms. The work addresses a significant knowledge gap regarding full-scale compartment fire scenarios relevant to both BESS and residential applications, as data on NMC-specific battery fire hazards in confined compartments remain limited. The present study does not follow the ISO 9705 procedure [30], which is a standard test method designed for assessing surface materials under controlled fire exposure; the ISO 9705 room configuration and measurement setup are adopted to provide a useful reference framework. The fire initiation deviates from the standard protocol.

The novelty lies in the quantitative characterisation of NMC-specific fire hazard parameters in compartment fires, providing quantitative data on fire growth, heat release rate, and room thermal exposure, as well as analysis of fire development phases and applicability of the ISO 834 time-temperature curve.

The outcomes of this study provide knowledge for fire safety engineering practice. The quantitative fire hazard data can be used to improve fire and explosion risk assessments of BESS installations, support the development of design guidelines in confined compartments, and complement existing testing protocols.
A total of 3 experiments were conducted on a LIB battery module in a room. Additional concrete elements were placed in the room for subsequent analysis of the effects of heat and gas exposure.

The experiments were conducted in a compartment at the laboratory at RISE Fire Research in Norway. The test compartment was a room with a floor area of 8.6 m2 and a ceiling height of 2.5 m according to the standard ISO 9705 [30]. The room walls, ceiling and floor are constructed of concrete, with no additional protection. The instrumentation mounted in the smoke exhaust duct was calibrated following the procedures described in the standard.

The room was ventilated solely by a doorway (2 m high, 0.8 m wide) at the centre of one of the 2.4 m wide walls (See Fig. 1 ). The origin of coordinates (X = 0, Y = 0, Z = 0) is represented in Fig. 1 (Top view). The doorway was fully open during all the experiments. The combustion products were collected by an exhaust hood, and samples were taken from the duct and directed to a gas analyser for gas measurements and calculation of HRR. An exhaust air flow of 1.8 ± 0.2 m3/s was kept constant during the experiments. The dimensions of the hood are 3 m × 3 m, and the hood is situated above the door opening. The ambient temperature in the room during the experiments was 10 ± 2 °C.

The specifications of the LIB module are given in Table 1 . The LIB module consisted of 28 NMC pouch cells connected in series, and each cell was separately placed with thermal insulating materials between them (see Fig. 3). It is important to note that the investigated modules are designed to prevent cell-to-cell propagation in their normal configuration. In this study, the coolant (e.g. liquid used in the cooling system to absorb and transfer heat from a module) was drained from the module to increase the likelihood of propagation. However, the physical structure of the cooling system (i.e., cooling channels) was still present in the module. Further actions to promote propagation are described in section 2.2.1. Each pouch cell contains 64 Ah and is designed with a flexible shell, a nominal voltage of 3.68V and a maximum voltage (OCV) of 4.182 V at 100% SoC. The battery module has a nominal total electrical energy stored of 6600 Wh, a nominal voltage of 103 Vnom, and a maximum voltage (OCV) of 117 V at 100% SoC, according to the manufacturer's product specifications.

During sample preparation, the LIB module was charged to the nominal voltage using a constant current (Charged until 117.1 V at 0.5 C-rate) and a constant voltage method (continued to charge at 117.1V until the current is < 3.2 A). The preparation ensures that all cells have comparable and defined conditions of SoC, OCV, and temperature. Each LIB module was charged to 100% SoC at the first cycle and discharged until the end of discharge voltage (EODV). Then, the LIB module was charged to 100% SoC at the second cycle and rested for a maximum of 8h before the start of the test. The first cycle is a conditioning step, while the second cycle ensures a consistent electrochemical state in the cells prior to TR experiments. The maximum rest period of 8 h was chosen to ensure electrochemical and thermal stabilization of the cells prior to testing. In the resting period, the module was not actively charged or discharged. The voltage of the LIB modules was measured via the battery management system (BMS); however, the measurement stopped once the corresponding cell entered TR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Location of LIB module and structural elements in the room
ce:section.ce:para: The positions of the LIB module and the structural elements in the room are illustrated in Fig. 1. The module was horizontally positioned, in line with how it would be placed in its final installation within a battery unit, to simulate the conditions it would experience during normal operation. The LIB module was situated at the centre of the room at a height of 0.7m above the floor, supported by three lightweight solid concrete blocks arranged in a vertical configuration. The venting area of the LIB module was oriented towards the concrete structural elements. In addition, two concrete boards and two concrete cubes, both pure concrete, non-fire-sealed, were mounted in the room. The concrete boards were affixed to steel frames and were separated by an insulation layer comprising mineral wool. The analysis of the condition and properties of the concrete elements after the battery fire experiments is presented in another article [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: TR trigger and test protocol during and after the experiment
ce:section.ce:para: A 40 kW propane burner, adapted from previous studies (Section 1.1), was employed to trigger TR in the battery module. This approach had previously shown good reproducibility. More importantly, it ensures a fast initial TR propagation. The TR propagation in a real BESS depends highly on the module design and failure mode. However, it can be expected that battery modules which limit TR to a single cell or allow only very slow propagation will not challenge a well-designed battery room. The employed trigger method ensures that several cells, about one third, reach TR simultaneously, while the remaining cells provide the possibility for the TR to propagate from cell to cell. The burner was positioned beneath the LIB module at the centre. The trigger method was identical for all experiments. However, in Experiments 2 and 3, a pilot flame was added in proximity to the structural concrete elements to prevent an accumulation of combustible gases and delayed ignition. The propane burner flame heated the cells via heat conduction through the casing. Hence, several cells are heated and forced into TR simultaneously. This was done to promote propagation in the remaining cells and is based on a study conducted by Meraner et al. [32]. The burner was turned off once the ignition of the off-gases was confirmed. Hence, the duration of the trigger method depended on the ignition time and not the time to TR.

All experiments were conducted in accordance with safety protocols to protect personnel and mitigate exposure to toxic gases and particulates released during TR. The exhaust system was monitored as described in section 2.1.1. The sampled gases and temperature monitoring (see section 2.3) were performed remotely, and no personnel were allowed near the experimental facility. From the start of the propane burner, the measures were taken as described in section 2.3. After the test, the burned LIB module was relocated to an area designated for the control of potential re-ignition. The concrete slab and concrete cube samples were stored for subsequent analyses. Later, the compartment was cleaned, and the data were analysed by the methods described in section 2.4.

Temperature and incident radiant heat flux at different locations inside the compartment were measured with thermocouples (TC) of type K 1.5 mm [33] and plate thermometers (PT) [34]. TCs were situated on the surface of the cells arranged within the LIB module and distributed in the room. The PTs measure surface temperatures of materials with good insulation characteristics.

Fig. 3 illustrates the configuration of the LIB module components and the distribution of TCs inside the module. The different components in the module are battery cells, plastic holders, a plastic cover with a ventilation opening and a metal casing. The metal casing is made of steel, while the plastic components are engineering thermoplastics with thermal insulation properties. The surface temperature of the cells was gauged using a total of 28 TCs distributed within the module, named TC_Batt1 to TC_Batt28. The dimensions of the cells are specified in Table 1. As illustrated in Fig. 3 side view, the temperature measurement point for each cell was situated as indicated by distances A and B. The distance A was 50 mm ± 5 mm from the top horizontal cell edge, while distance B was 176 mm ± 10 mm from either the right (from cell 1 to 14) or left (from cell 15 to 28) vertical cell edge. The different locations of TCs from the vertical cell edge were due to the wiring of the TCs exiting through the ventilation opening for easier installation and access. The TR process was monitored by measuring the temperature rise at the cell surface using thermocouples mounted inside the module. The module was replaced after each experiment, so each experiment used a new battery module and new TCs within the module.

A total of 21 TCs were used to measure the temperature of the gases in the compartment. 7 TCs were mounted on three TC trees that were positioned as shown in Fig. 1 and in Fig. 2
. The TCs of the first tree were designated TC_TreeJet, and were situated at a distance of 0.2m from the wall with the concrete elements and 2.2m from the door. The TCs of the second tree were designated TC_TreeMiddle, and were placed near the centre of the room, at 1.2m from both the right and the left walls and 2.2 m from the door. The TCs of the third tree were designated TC_TreeBack, and were placed at a distance of 0.2 m from the back wall and 3.4m from the door. The heights of the TCs (Z positions) are also shown in Table 2 . The identification (ID) of thermocouple trees corresponds to the tree name and the Z position (e.g. TC_TreeJet_10 is the thermocouple placed at the Tree_Jet at Z = 2.3 m from the floor level). The standard temperature range of TCs was from −200 °C to +1250 °C. The uncertainty of the temperature measurements was ±2.2 °C in the relevant range (15 – 375 °C) or ±0.75% of the measured value according to the laboratory calibration. At high temperatures, additional uncertainty is expected from the heat transfer effect (both convective and radiative) on the thermocouples, as reported in previous studies on thermocouple performance in fire environments [35].

5 PTs were mounted at different locations within the compartment in order to estimate the incident radiant heat flux resulting from the LIB battery module fire. The PTs were manufactured by spot welding a 0.2 mm type K TC to a 10 cm × 10 cm, 0.7 mm thick steel sheet. The steel sheet was backed with 10 mm insulation of type FireMaster Marine Plus. See Fig. 2 and details of the PT locations can be found in Table 2.
The mass loss of the LIB modules was measured during the experiments. The LIB module was placed at the top of lightweight concrete blocks on a mass scale. The scale had an uncertainty of ±0.19 kg.

The instrumentation of the exhaust duct was according to ISO 9705. Concentrations of carbon dioxide (CO2), carbon monoxide (CO) and oxygen (O2) were measured by an X-STREAM instrument, including a paramagnetic oxygen gas analyser and a non-dispersive infrared (NDIR) sensor for CO2 and CO.

Data was recorded at 1-s intervals until all temperatures within the battery module fell below 100 °C. The experiment was recorded by three video cameras, including an IR camera. Two GoPro cameras (named GoPro front and GoPro back) were placed in two of the room corners within cylindrical glass bottles filled with ambient temperature water (See Fig. 1). The IR camera was placed in front of the room door. Video recordings were used to evaluate the extent of flying debris and flame heights. The IR camera was used to assess gas venting, both before and after the initial TR event.

ce:section.ce:para:

Tg was estimated using the 1.5 mm TC positioned near each PT. TPT measure a surface temperature that reflects the combined effects of radiative and convective heat transfer. This temperature approximates the adiabatic surface temperature and typically lies between the gas temperature and the radiative temperature of the surroundings. The value of gas temperature (Tg) for the PTs (PTC_WL, PTC_C, PTC_M and PTC_J) were calculated based on the readings from the corresponding TCs located at TC_LPF, TC_C, TC_TreeMiddle_1.55, and TC_LPF, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method to determine heat release rate
ce:section.ce:para: The HRR from the fire was determined through two distinct methods: oxygen consumption calorimetry (OCC) and carbon dioxide generation (CDG). The use of OCC in battery fire scenarios is subject to several uncertainties. These include contributions from self-generated oxygen, internal heat generation (IHG), the variability of the effective heat of combustion (E-factor), and the complexity of total gas release during combustion events. These factors significantly influence the reliability of HRR measurements in small-scale tests. In TR scenarios, especially in single-cell tests, the rapid heat release associated with cell failure can lead to underestimations of the HRR peak by as much as 100%, as shown by Willstrand et al. [37]. However, the inclusion of surrounding combustible components in large battery systems decreases the relative errors in the estimation of HRR peak. Furthermore, OCC will exclude the IHG and may underestimate the total heat release (THR) of external flaming combustion by up to 10% in battery cells, yet this margin of uncertainty is comparable to that already inherent in OCC, and in large-scale battery fires it may be smaller. The self-generated oxygen will mainly contribute to the IHG. Furthermore, it is not advised to implement CO correction in the OCC method, as the CO gas released from the battery has the potential to induce significant errors. In contrast, the CDG method accounts for both the CO2 and CO emissions from the battery, and the method is potentially suitable to evaluate the THR, including the IHG [37].

In full-scale experiments, the heat release rate
q ˙
from the product and ignition burner estimated by the OCC method is using equations (2) and (3), see Janssens et al. [38]. A similar approach is included in the standard ISO 9705-1 annexes [30].
