The analysis focuses on the temperature development within LIB module during TR, fire development in the room, room temperatures, heat fluxes, gas measurements, heat release rate and mass loss. The findings are discussed in Section 4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature development within the LIB module during thermal runaway
ce:section.ce:para: This section presents an overview of the experiment series results, including the time to TR and the TR onset temperature for selected battery cells. The present study used a multicriterion to identify the TR onset, similar to Meraner et al. [40]. The multicriterion is based on temperature measurements similar to the work by Bruchhausen et al. [41], who found that criteria based on the temperature gradient and a reference temperature yielded more accurate results than those based on combining the temperature gradient and cell voltage. The TR onset region was identified as the region with a temperature above 100 °C for at least 10 s and a temperature gradient (dT/dt) of at least 10 °C/s. The TR onset was then marked as the first prominent peak in the second derivative of the temperature, within that region. The temperature gradient threshold is higher than the 1.67 °C/s threshold used by Sanfeliu Meliá et al. [42] for the same cells. This was done to avoid false positives in the TR onset detection, as the temperature data from module tests exposed to an external flame are inherently noisier than those from single-cell tests with electrical heating. However, 10 °C/s is still within the typical range of threshold values reported in the literature, which range from 1 °C/s to 15 °C/s [43–45]. Furthermore, the identified TR events were confirmed by visual observation of video recordings in the visible and infrared spectra. The detailed study of TR propagation in single cells, such as cell TR onset temperature and time to TR, and within the module, is outside the scope of the present work. However, a general understanding of the TR initiation in specific individual cells, the ignition of gases within the room and cell-to-cell TR propagation is crucial for explaining the fire development in the room. Fig. 4 illustrates the TR propagation timeline (in minutes) following the first TR event for all experiments. The number of cells undergoing TR prior to room gas ignition was approximately 19 cells in Experiment 2, and around 10 cells in both Experiments 1 and 3.

Fig. 5 illustrates the surface temperature development of selected cells (e.g. TC_batt1, TC_batt2, TC_batt10, TC_batt19, TC_batt20, and TC_batt27 for experiment 1) within the battery module in all experiments. The selected cells were chosen to represent the range of thermal responses observed among the 28 monitored cells. These illustrations capture the variation in temperature evolution across different locations within the module. Cells exhibiting measurement noise or overlapping signals that could interfere with the clear visualization of other temperature traces were excluded to preserve readability. According to Fig. 5, the shortest time to TR was observed at approximately. 12 min for cells 13 to 19 in Experiment 1, at 21 min for cells 7 to 18 in Experiment 2, and at 14 min for cells 9 to 18 in Experiment 3. The TR onset temperature ranged from 120 to 250 °C in Experiment 1, from 100 to 200 °C in Experiment 2, and from 100 to 220 °C in Experiment 3. The first cells to undergo TR were TC_batt19 in Experiment 1, TC_batt13 in Experiment 2, and TC_batt13 in Experiment 3, as determined through analysis of cell temperature data.

The fire development for all experiments is detailed below, with a summary of Experiment 1 in Table 3 . This table collect the observations captured by the cameras and the corresponding figures at different times. The duration of the observations, in [min: sec], are also summarised for all experiments in Table 4 . The battery modules were exposed to the burner flame similarly in all the experiments. The burner flame was removed once the gas ignited. The exact burner flame exposure durations were 20:30 min (e.g. 20 min and 30 s) in Experiment 1, 21:60 min in Experiment 2 and 13:55 min in Experiment 3.

Experiment 2 – A total of 7 jet flames was identified from the videos. The initial gas venting and formation of a gas layer beneath the ceiling developed similarly to Experiment 1. However, unlike Experiment 1, gas outflow through the door was not clearly visible before gas ignition. The first intense gas release occurred at 21:00 min. The gases were ignited by a pilot flame at 21:04 min, resulting in a stronger flash fire compared to Experiment 1 (see Fig. 8 a and b). 3 s later, the combustible gases ignited, causing smoke to flow out of the door opening (see Fig. 8b). The first jet flame was horizontal, followed by a diffusive flame that reached the right corner of the room. At 21:44 min, flames were pushed outside the room for 2 s (see Fig. 8d). A significant amount of material fell from the module and continued to burn on the floor, as observed in Experiment 1. The sequence of jet flames was consistent with Experiment 1 (see Fig. 8f). The fire was self-extinguished at 91:00 min.

Experiment 3 – A total of 12 jet flames was identified from the videos. The overall fire development was like that observed in Experiment 1. However, gas ignition occurred earlier in Experiment 3; at 13:54 min. The duration of the flash fire was shorter than for Experiment 1 and Experiment 2. However, the duration of the first horizontal jet flame was longer. The fire self-extinguished at 80:30 min.

The gas temperatures within the compartment were measured by TCs, while the radiative-convective temperatures were measured by PTs, as shown in Figs. 1 and 2. The gas temperatures recorded by the three thermocouple trees (TreeJet, TreeMiddle and TreeBack, designated a), b) and c) respectively), ISO 834 fire curve and hydrocarbon fire curve are plotted for all experiments in Fig. 9 . The temperatures as a function of time are compared for all experiments at various locations. The thermocouples, at 230 cm, 130 cm and 80 cm, were chosen as they were located near the ceiling, at mid-room height, and close the height of the battery. Shaded areas in the plots represent temperature ranges 230 cm to 80 cm. Table 5 summarizes by experiment ID, thermocouple tree ID, thermocouple position at Z (in cm), maximum temperature at gas ignition, and duration of the threshold above 500 °C in [min:sec]. A time interval during which the upper gas layer temperature remains above a threshold of approximately 500 °C is often associated with the onset of flashover in compartment fire studies [46], which is discussed later. From Fig. 9, the maximum temperatures in Experiment 1 were lower than in Experiments 2 and 3, and occurred at room gas ignition. The position of maximum temperatures was, for Tree_Jet, at mid-room height (at 130 cm) and close to the height of the battery (at 80 cm). Moreover, maximum temperatures for Tree_Middle and Tree_Back were recorded by the thermocouples located near the ceiling (at 230 cm). Although gas temperatures exceeded the 500 °C threshold in all experiments, the exposure duration above this level was less than 1 min. Fig. 10 shows the incident heat radiation of two PTs, respectively PTC_WL and PTC_C, for all experiments.

The incident heat radiation (kW/m2) for each PT was estimated using the temperature measurement from the plate thermometer and the reference gas temperature. Details on reference points are in section 2.4.1. The heat received by an object positioned in front of the jet flame was estimated using PTC_WL. The heat exposure parallel to the jet flame, directed towards the ceiling, and up toward the module were represented by PTC_J, PTC_C, and PTC_M (see Fig. 2). Measurements from PTC_WH were solely used in the assessment of the condition of the concrete elements [31] and are not presented in this article. Table 6 collects the experiment results per PT, with key parameters such as the maximum measured temperature [°C], heat flux peak [kW/m2], time of heat flux peak [min:sec], and duration of threshold above 20 kW/m2 [min: sec]. A sustained heat flux above approximately 20 kW/m2 is considered sufficient to potentially ignite surrounding combustible materials, although the threshold depends on material properties and exposure conditions [46]. Negative incident heat radiation can occur when intense fire exposure ceases abruptly, initiating a cooling phase for the heated plate thermometer. However, negative values were not identified in the data.

The concentrations of carbon monoxide (CO), carbon dioxide (CO2) and oxygen (O2) were measured throughout all experiments. Fig. 11 illustrates the measured concentrations (vol %) of gas CO2 and CO in the exhaust pipe of the calorimeter. However, a data recording error in the gas analyser for Experiment 3 prevented the measurement of gas levels. Experiments 1 and 2 revealed notable differences in gas volume percentages before and during battery combustion. The fire at gas ignition in Experiment 2 was more intense, as described in section 3.3, with gas released ignited within the room between 800 and 1000 °C compared to gas ignition in the range 400 – 850 °C in Experiment 1 (See Fig. 9). Before gas ignition, CO2 and CO concentrations differed significantly between the experiments. In Experiment 2, CO2 and CO levels were approximately 0.1 vol% and 0.01 vol%, respectively, whereas in Experiment 1 they were higher, around 0.2 vol% and 0.1 vol%. These elevated levels in Experiment 1 resulted from some cells undergoing TR prior to ignition, producing significant gas emissions before flaming. Gas concentrations at and after gas ignition were also presented. In Experiment 2, the CO2 increased to 4 vol%, while the oxygen level was reduced from 21 vol% to 16 vol%. The concentrations of CO increased significantly at the ignition of the room gases, reaching peak levels of 0.5 vol%. In contrast, Experiment 1 showed lower CO2 concentration, ranging between 1 and 0.5 vol%, CO levels remained below 0.1 vol% and oxygen levels reduced to 19.8%. However, the release of CO2 was more intermittent over time.

CO/CO2 ratio was evaluated over the entire experimental duration. Before gas ignition, Experiment 1 exhibited a high ratio of 0.45 compared to 0.01 in Experiment 2. Combustion efficiency, assessed after ignition based on the CO/CO2 ratio, was similar in both experiments, with a value of 0.12.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat release rate and mass loss
ce:section.ce:para: The HRR is presented in Fig. 12 for Experiment 1 and Fig. 13 for Experiment 2, and was estimated by the OCC and CDG methods, as described in section 2.4.2. Figs. 12 and 13 also plot diff, which is the difference between OCC and CDG measurements. The experiments demonstrated differences in HRR curves. For Experiment 1, the HRR curve showed several peaks lasting approximately 1 min, with similar steep increase and decrease, correlating with the observed jet flames (see sections 3.3 and 3.4). In contrast, Experiment 2 exhibited a very steep peak of HRR followed by smaller peaks. Although an error in the gas analyser prevented the calculation of the HRR curve for Experiment 3, the sequence of energy release was expected to be similar to the HRR curve of Experiment 1, as they exhibited a comparable number of temperature peaks over time (see Fig. 9).

For Experiment 1 and Experiment 2, the peak HRR by OCC was 462.1 kW and 1605.7 kW, respectively, and the peak HRR by CDG was 430.9 kW and 1723.2 kW. The HRR curves plotted using the OCC and CDG methods showed consistent overall trends throughout the experiments, though some relative discrepancies in magnitude were observed. In some cases, the OCC method provided a higher HRR value compared to the CDG measurements (see Figs. 12 and 13). Additionally, Fig. 14 represents the HRR measured using both methods, alongside the t-squared HRR curve representing an ultra-fast fire for the experiments. This curve represents a t-squared fire, where HRR is proportional to
(
t k
)
2
, t is time (min) and
k
is a growth constant of 1.25 min/√MW (or equivalent 75 s/√MW) [47]. The curve is aligned with the observed time to ignition in each experiment. The growth constants were estimated assuming a reference HRR of 1 MW in order to enable direct comparison with standard fire growth classifications. Based on this normalization, the estimated growth constants were
k = 1.0
min/√MW for Experiment 1 and
k = 0.5
min/√MW for Experiment 2.

The mass loss during the fire experiments was determined as described in section 2.3. Table 7 presents the initial mass, final mass, total mass loss (TML) in percentage, total heat release (THR) by OCC and CDG, and the effective heat of combustion by OCC and CDG recorded for the experiments. The highest mass loss percentage was for Experiment 3, with a 46.5 %, in comparison with a 33.3 % for Experiment 1 and a 36.4 % for Experiment 2. All cells experienced TR in all the experiments. The area under the HRR curve was used to calculate the total energy released. The CDG method provided a higher value of THR for both experiments. 85% of the total energy was released during 6 min for Experiment 2, while the energy was released more evenly over around 30 min for Experiment 1. The effective heat of combustion was estimated by the THR and mass loss [48].

Variability was observed among the repeated fire experiments; however, its degree varied between parameters. Main differences were regarding (1) the cells contributing to the initial gas vent, and (2) the time to ignition of gases within the compartment. The initial cells are, in the present work, defined as all cells that entered TR due to the trigger method and not due to cell-to-cell TR propagation.
(1)
The number of cells that entered TR simultaneously varied between experiments, with two experiments showing a comparable number of initially triggered cells (10 cells, roughly 30% of the module), while one experiment (Experiment 2) showed a significantly larger number of cells contributing to the initial TR (19 cells, 60% of the module). The number of initial cells in TR is linked to the TR onset time, which was longer in Experiment 2, providing more time for the preheating of cells, bringing them closer to their onset temperature. The TR onset temperatures in single NMC-cell experiments conducted by the present authors under inert atmosphere conditions were in the range of 300 - 380 °C [42], while experiments performed in an open environment showed around 280 - 300 °C [9]. In comparison, the cell surface temperatures at TR onset observed in the module experiments were considerably lower, ranging between 100 °C and 200 °C (See section 3.1), which can be explained by the thermocouple placement. In cell-level experiments, it is possible to place thermocouples at the location with the highest heating rate. In module-level experiments, especially with external trigger methods, it is not feasible to precisely anticipate the location of the highest heating rate.

The range of TR onset temperatures among individual cells during the module experiments was also considerable. The variability is associated with heating rate effects, the trigger method and thermal boundary conditions. The external heating applied to the base of the battery's metal casing generated intense conductive heating. The TR onset in individual cells was influenced by both localized heating and heat transfer from neighbouring cells already experiencing elevated temperatures, leading to different heating rates across cells. Fig. 15 shows the analysis of the heating rates experienced by the battery cells prior to the first TR, focusing on the cells which contributed to the large difference between Experiment 2 and the other two experiments. The maximum difference in the average heating rate until the first TR between Experiment 2 and Experiment 1, as well as between Experiment 2 and Experiment 3, was 1.15 °C/min and 1.02 °C/min. The difference between Experiment 1 and Experiment 3 was only 0.09 °C/min. This, in combination with a longer preheating time, has the potential to affect the outcome considerably. However, based on the available data and the limited number of repetitions, it is not possible to conclude that the increased heating rate in Experiment 2 is the sole cause of the different propagation behaviour.

This study highlights that TR initiation is sensitive to variations in the degree of cell pre-heating from an external burner. However, the present study does not include data on burner-on vs. burner-off effects, and this interpretation cannot be confirmed based on available data. Both our work and Bisschop et al. [49] turned off the burner once the gases ignited. Other studies (e.g., Ditch et al. [21], Barrowy et al. [28]) investigated similar chemistries in free burn large-scale experiments; however, repeating the same experiment with the same trigger method was not the goal of their studies. Bisschop et al. [49] demonstrated relatively consistent cell-to-cell TR propagation in NMC modules triggered by an external gas burner, noting that module design and burner placement influenced the experimental repeatability. In our study, the cell-to-cell propagation after gas ignition/turning off the burner, i.e., during the later stage, showed good repeatability between experiments. As discussed in section 4.5, TC data for cells 22 to 28 in Experiment 1 are excluded due to poor TC placement (see Fig. 4).
(2)
The time to gas ignition differed between experiments, see “the vertical edge at 0” in Fig. 4, which marks the time after the first TR occurred. In Experiment 2, more cells were heated and vented gases during the initial TR, resulting in a higher concentration of flammable gases within the room. A larger number of pre-heated cells at the time of ignition results in a more energetic initial jet flame and a faster HRR (A factor of 3.5 variation in peak HRR).

The concentration of the hot gases and the presence of a sustained pilot flame were key factors influencing the time to ignition. The difference in ignition delay cannot be evaluated between Experiment 1 and the others, as only Experiment 1 was conducted without a pilot flame. In Exp 1, ignition of flammable gases was probably caused by localised electrical sparking or a short circuit within the module. In contrast, in the Experiment 2 and 3, ignition was attributed to the presence of a pilot flame and the accumulation of sufficient gases within the room. Despite that, no consistent correlation was found between time to ignition and the presence of a pilot flame. These results present the increased hazard associated with delayed ignition, as gas accumulation may reach the potential for deflagration in compartments, as Barrowy et al. [23,50] demonstrated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fire development phases in a compartment with LIBs
ce:section.ce:para: The fire development in a compartment containing li-ion battery modules is described in terms of growth, fully developed and decay phases, consistent with the terminology commonly used in the analysis of compartment fires involving conventional building combustibles such as plastics, fabrics and wood. Table 8 collects fire development metrics from the experimental series. Fire development phases were identified based on the temporal evolution of HRR and room temperature measurements. The approach provides a practical framework to describe the fire evolution, while acknowledging that the identified phases are descriptive and should not be interpreted as equivalent to the phases defined in compartment fire theory.

In traditional compartment fires (e.g., with conventional combustible materials such as plastics, wood), the fire growth phase is governed by surface pyrolysis and flaming combustion of solid fuels, by room ventilation and by room geometry. The fire growth rate is usually characterised using a parabolic curve known as t-squared fire. This curve is used to construct pre-flashover design fires in rooms and defines the time to reach a reference HRR, as presented Drysdale [43], and Karlsson [44]. In addition to room factors, the fire growth in compartments with LIBs is related to the amount of accumulated flammable gases (e.g., volatile organic components from electrolyte vapours), the cell-to-cell TR propagation rate within the module, and the time to ignition of combustible gases. Two distinct phases were identified from the conducted experiments: (1) a slow heat acceleration phase, characterised by the venting of flammable gases from individual cells under the TR process (pre-heating, self-heating and TR stage), and (2) a rapid heat acceleration phase following the gas ignition.

Before flaming, substantial amounts of flammable gases were released from the individual cells under the TR process. The acceleration of heat release was driven by the simultaneous TR of multiple cells upon gas ignition. This effect was reflected in the variation of HRR peaks between experiments (See Figs. 12 and 13). Once ignition occurred, the fire growth proceeded with an accelerated heat release rate as more individual cells became involved. Following the first HRR peak, the cell-to-cell TR propagation became more linear.

A theoretical t-squared fire curve of a growth constant of 1.25 min/√MW (ultra-fast fire growth rate) was compared with the experimental HRR curves. The growth constant was also estimated per experiment. Results revealed that the ultra-fast fire classification (1.25 min/√MW) may not adequately capture the observed growth rates of compartments with LIB installations.

Simultaneous TR of nearly all cells within a module is a realistic fire scenario in the event of an external fire, such as a cabling fire, where the flames engulf the battery modules. In case ignition is caused by the module itself, such as an internal short circuit, all cells in TR at the same time are unlikely. Additionally, while a larger number of actively venting cells can increase the available fuel, factors such as the flammability limits of gas mixtures and room ventilation conditions significantly influence the magnitude of heat release. Xie et al. [51] demonstrated that the lower flammability limit of gases emitted during TR events can be as low as 10%. Combined with limited ventilation and potential ignition sources, the probability of gas ignition after gas release is high.

In traditional compartment fires, the fully developed phase is characterised by a sustained HRR, uniform flame spread across combustibles, and elevated compartment temperatures. This typically occurs under a ventilation-controlled regime and continues until fuel depletion [52,53].

The start of the fully developed phase in the experimental room was characterised by the maximum HRR, and the driving of successive energy bursts and jet flames caused by cell-to-cell TR propagation. The energy bursts produced multiple HRR peaks, enhancing the non-uniform combustion process. The behaviour aligns with Mao et al. [16], who observed accelerated TR propagation and exponential increases in HRR peaks in enclosed module clusters compared to open configurations. The energy released also varied with the specific safety measures integrated within the enclosure, such as variations in the thickness of the cell thermal barrier plates. In our study, both thermal barriers and the air layer between cells slowed down conductive heat transfer within the module, particularly after gas ignition. This slowing indicates that the cell-to-cell TR propagation is influenced by a localised heat accumulation in individual cells not yet in TR. In addition, the integrated safety measures (e.g. insulating and spacing) between cells affect the fire development phase, and further investigation is required to quantify their effects.

The fire remained in the fuel-controlled regime in the experiments, as the available oxygen was not limiting the fire. The oxygen concentrations were above 16% in the gas layer of the compartment measured at the exhaust duct. Also, the HRR continued the rate of fuel release from the battery cells. The CO/CO2 ratio after ignition was similar for the experiments. The production of CO2 was approximately ten times higher than CO, which indicates that combustion remained efficient. Sustained flaming was observed in the experiments and associated with the progress of TR in battery cells. This supports that the fire was governed by the availability of combustible gases released during TR. An alternative interpretation of the classical “fully developed phase” for batteries might be the term “intermittent flaming with multiple peaks”; however, the terminology is consistent with fire safety engineering practice.

The experiments revealed that gas temperatures near the ceiling exceeded 500 °C at gas ignition, and remained above this level for 30 to 60 s. The measured temperature was spatially limited to either one, two or three upper thermocouples (2.3, 2.05, and 1.8 cm) in each of the three arrays (Fig. 2) and exhibited a short-duration peak coinciding with gas ignition. For example, in Experiment 2 and 3, three adjacent upper sensors at 2.3 cm recorded a simultaneous temperature increase exceeding 500 °C, with similar values and a visible smoke production, which may represent the temperature of the upper gas layer. Smoke production was also visible in Experiment 1; however, no simultaneous readings were recorded by the three upper sensors at 2.3 cm, indicating a more localised thermal response. In Experiment 2, in addition to gas temperatures exceeding 500 °C near the ceiling, the conditions observed in Fig. 8d), including flames emerging from the doorway and an HRR exceeding 1 MW, are consistent with the occurrence of flashover in the room. In Experiment 1 and Experiment 3 were identified as not reaching flashover. Flashover has previously been observed during fire tests involving overcharged e-scooters in closed bedrooms [54]. It is reasonable to assume that, in the presence of other modules within the same compartment, there is a significant risk of fire spreading to them. However, further investigation is required to confirm this.

Localised flame impingement occurred at the centre of the ceiling and on the wall directly facing the module's ventilation opening (See Fig. 7). Measured incident heat on the ceiling (PTC_C) and a wall (PTC_WL) exceeded 20 kW/m2. These values are comparable to the critical heat flux thresholds for spontaneous and piloted ignition of halogen electrical cables, as reported by Courty et al. [55], and for halogen-free electrical cables, as estimated by Meinier et al. [56]. Although the heat exposure was intermittent, it was significant (see Table 6), with both duration and intensity varying by sensor location and experiment. A secondary ignition of surrounding combustibles was not assessed in this work; the thermal conditions measured are representative of those capable of initiating fires in nearby components. In practical battery rooms, nearby components such as adjacent battery modules, battery management systems units (BMS), power conversion units, and distributed cabling installed in cable trays may be exposed to similar heat fluxes and gas temperatures, being vulnerable to ignition or damage. These components are often housed in polymer enclosures or insulated with materials susceptible to thermal degradation and ignition. As examples, a post incident-analysis by Conzen et al. [57] and experimental research by Barowy et al. [23] have documented such secondary fire propagation within battery rooms.

In traditional compartment fires, the decay phase is governed by the depletion of available fuel, with a reduction in room temperatures and combustion intensity. The transition into the decay phase in the present experiments was identified by the drop of HRR below 100 kW for Experiment 1 and 200 kW for Experiment 2, and compartment temperatures below 250 °C. Given the fuel-controlled nature of LIB fires, these values are used as practical indicators of phase transition rather than strict criteria. The criterion was based on the observation that approximately 60 to 70% of the cells within the module had undergone TR, which occurred 20 min after the first cells undergoing TR/gas ignition (see Fig. 5). The remaining cells entered TR later, but their energy release was considerably lower, as it originated from individual cells rather than multiple cells simultaneously. The measured HRR of approx. 100 kW aligns with the expected output from a single 63Ah NMC cell undergoing TR, as observed Gagnon et al. in small-scale open fire tests [9]. The decay phase showed an irregular pattern, with distinct HRR peak values. These peaks were attributed to the continuous gas venting, intermittent small flames, and smouldering combustion, caused by the individual cells that experienced late TR. Residual combustibles from vented gases, such as condensed electrolyte vapours, separator materials, and plastic casing, may have sustained smouldering. Although reignition was not observed, previous free-burning tests of battery modules in enclosures have demonstrated the potential for reignition [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat release: oxygen consumption vs carbon dioxide generation
ce:section.ce:para: As previously shown in section 2.4.2, on the cell level, OCC may underestimate the HRR peak by up to 100% and this is primarily due to the nature of the TR event, which creates a high gas production rate and violent combustion. The uncertainty of the HRR peak for module level should be smaller as more fuels, such as plastics, are involved in the combustion, which are better suited for OCC, although this uncertainty has not been quantified [37]. Willstrand et al. [37] stated that if the gas production rate is much higher than the flow gas rate in the exhaust stream, both methods, OCC and CDG, will smear HRR data, leading to longer durations and lower peaks.

In this work, the variation in HRR curves and the large differences in the first HRR peak value between experiments were primarily attributed to the number of cells undergoing TR simultaneously at gas ignition. An important aspect is the observed long duration of gas venting without ignition in Experiment 1. During unignited TR, large quantities of gases (CO2, CO, H2, CH4, etc.) are released by the decomposition of cell internal components rather than external combustion. Despite the absence of combustion, both methods registered the venting as HRR. This is a limitation of calorimetric methods. The release of CO2 from the cells reduced the relative O2 concentration. The CDG method overpredicted the HRR compared to the OCC, because it includes the internal heat generation (IHG) from the production of CO2 and CO. The large CO2 release will also result in a larger value for the CDG method than for the OCC, as was seen in the present work during the unignited venting in Experiment 1. Neither method provides a meaningful HRR during unignited TR, although both methods remain useful for identifying the onset of the TR stage.

After gas ignition, corresponding to the fire growth phase, both OCC and CDG followed the same trend. The Diff plot in Figs. 12 and 13 showed that the relative distance, defined as the relative deviation of CDG with respect to OCC, remains within 5–10% during periods of sustained battery burning. However, during short transient events, the relative distance increases significantly. For Experiment 1, the relative distance ranging from −40% → +20% is during the most intense fire period between 19 min and 21 min, whereas for Experiment 2. from −20% → +30% between 21 min and 23 min. The variations between methods were attributed to chemical phenomena during TR, including IHG from self-generated oxygen reacting within the module (captured by CDG but not by OCC), incomplete combustion (e.g. CO/CO2 variations), and a potential secondary oxidation in the extract duct. OCC may underestimate HRR due to intermittent combustion, while CDG may overestimate HRR due to contributions from IHG and incomplete combustion. A complementary use of both methods provides a more informative interpretation of the energy release during the fire growth phase.

In the fully developed phase, combustion is dominated by conventional materials within the battery module and by additional cells undergoing TR. Under these conditions, the contribution of IHG becomes small relative to the total energy released, and OCC provides a robust estimate of HRR, whereas CDG remains more sensitive to variations in combustion efficiency.

In the decay phase, for the present study conducted with an open door, combustion remained relatively complete, as indicated by low CO/CO2 ratios, and occurred under oxygen-rich conditions despite the decreasing HRR. The transition to decay was primarily driven by fuel depletion rather than oxygen limitation. Under these conditions, both OCC and CDG yield reasonable estimates of the residual heat release.
The estimation of HRR should be interpreted cautiously during short fluctuations. Employing both methods provides a more comprehensive understanding of the energy release.

THR values were possible to compare between experiments, as THR integrates the heat output over the entire duration of the experiments, and it is associated with the total mass loss of the battery. The total mass loss varied between experiments, which indicates that some flammable components, such as electrolyte vapours, separator materials, and plastic housing, may not be disintegrated or burnt. The increase in THR was found to correlate with the increase in total mass loss. The THR estimated using the CDG method was slightly higher than that derived from OCC (see Table 7). However, the percentage magnitude of this difference did not provide a consistent trend that could be clearly correlated with the specific experimental conditions (e.g. cells undergoing TR, and thermal distribution).

The standardized time-temperature curves, ISO 834 and the hydrocarbon fire, were represented in Fig. 9 to provide a qualitative reference. Both curves represent the fully developed phase with continuous fuel supply and a temperature of a well-mixed gas layer. The experiments showed that a single-module fire generated a fast local gas temperature rise immediately after gas ignition. The progression from gas ignition through fire growth and into the fully developed phase occurred in less than 1 min (See Table 8). It is reasonable to compare our room temperatures with both standard curves when referenced from the time of gas ignition. As a result, the measured early temperature exceeded ISO 834 curve, whereas the hydrocarbon curve better reflected the rapid initial heating. However, ISO 834 provided a conservative thermal exposure during the later stage of the fire. This provides a useful, though illustrative, reference for potential design considerations.

The battery fire showed multiple jet flames associated with sequential TR events. The transient behaviour and localized temperature peaks are not captured by standard fire curves. The comparison between the gas temperature of the furnace (ISO 834) and local thermocouple measurements in the experimental room is inherently qualitative.

From a structural fire resistance perspective, a single-module fire involves a limited fire load. However, the presence of multiple modules could generate a more uniform and sustained gas layer temperature rise. In such scenarios, the hydrocarbon curve may more appropriately serve as a reference for structural design in battery rooms. This implies that the fire resistance requirements for structures housing multiple modules may need to consider more severe thermal exposure than those based solely on ISO 834. Further work is required to quantify such conditions.

Some experimental limitations, as the specific cell chemistry and SoC, the selected trigger method, compartment boundaries and uncertainty of measurements, may influence the interpretation and generalisation of the results. Also, the nature of fire experiments, often resulting in large variability in test results for repeated tests performed in close-to-identical test setups with the same operators and test material, represents a challenge to the analysis of test results. The variability is a result of known uncertainties related to measurements, but also connected to the unknown variability in test conditions, tested material (here: batteries), ventilation conditions, etc. A series of three experiments is too small to perform a statistical analysis of the results. However, we believe that the experiments and test results presented here will give enough information about the fire behaviour of li-ion batteries in a small enclosure to be able to design efficient fire safety measures. The experience from these tests may also be used as a basis for future experiments.

The selection of the trigger method was based on two criteria: ensuring cell-to-cell TR propagation and achieving a fire size that impacts the compartment environment. A gas burner was employed to supply a stable and repeatable external heat source to the battery cells. While this approach ensured consistent initiation, the experimental results were sensitive to the number of cells actively venting flammable gases during the initial stage. However, the chosen trigger method enabled cell-to-cell propagation to occur from a low external heat input (e.g. low-intensity external fire), achieving realistic TR initiation temperatures and representing plausible battery failure scenarios.

The compartment boundaries, such as the ventilation, the room geometry and the fuel bed configuration, limit the findings. The natural well-ventilation by the doorway ensured the limitation of oxygen depletion. The setup is representative of compartments with active or passive ventilation systems, but it may not reflect the behaviour in poorly ventilated compartments. The enclosed metal casing had a limited opening, so room ventilation had minimal effect on cell-to-cell TR propagation. Regarding the room geometry, small compartments (<10 m2) limit gas heat dissipation from the source, and increase the hot gas accumulation, intensifying radiative heat feedback to both the battery and compartment surfaces. This can increase the local temperature, accelerate the TR propagation, and influence the fire growth rate. Moreover, the spatial configuration of the battery module and its ventilation opening also had a notable effect on gas distribution, flame spread direction, and temperature distribution within the compartment. The thermal properties of enclosure materials may further influence the radiative heat feedback to the battery module. Materials with high thermal inertia, such as concrete, may result in lower radiative feedback during the fire growth phase than during the decay phase. However, this effect requires further investigation. It is believed that the cell-to-cell heat transfer was primarily governed by the heat transfer mechanisms within the module structure.

Measurement limitations are acknowledged in this work. The applicability of the OCC and CDG methods is limited during TR events that occur during non-flaming conditions (See section 4.3). Gas analysis was limited to CO2, CO and O2, without quantification of toxic fluorinated species such as HF. Regarding instrumentation, TCs were inserted between each cell and the plastic holder, positioned near the cell centre with a slide offset. Those embedded within the module were not fixed to the cell surface using a conductive adhesive. In Experiment 1, the thermocouple (TC) wires were routed from the cells towards the ventilation opening along the bottom of the module. Consequently, the wires were exposed to significant heating from the burner, particularly for the TCs located farthest from the ventilation opening. This led to early failures of several TCs and introduced considerable uncertainty in determining the TC-based TR onset. For instance, the TR onset for cells 23–28, as indicated by the cell temperature, did not correspond with the TR events observed using other means (HRR, video recordings, and room temperature measurements). The TC wires for Experiment 2 and 3 were therefore routed along the top of the module, providing better protection of the wires. This resulted in a cleaner signal, fewer failures and a more even initial temperature distribution reflecting the heat flux expected from the burner.

In addition, heat transfer effects contribute to the measurement uncertainty. The TCs were 1.5 mm Type-K, which have higher thermal mass and slower response time than smaller TCs. In high-velocity gas flows, such as battery jet flames, convective heat transfer may dominate, and the measured temperature may be closer to the gas temperature. However, radiative heat transfer can still introduce additional degrees. As a result, TCs exposed to the glowing flame regions and high velocity gas flow may record values between the gas temperature and the radiative temperature. This uncertainty is higher than for TCs stabilized close to the gas temperature, or to the radiative temperature. Furthermore, the estimation of incident heat radiation involves several assumptions related to ɛ, hc and Tg, which are temperature dependent. A brief sensitivity analysis was conducted by varying these parameters within realistic bounds, and the estimated incident heat radiation was within ±20%.

The study was limited to experiments involving a single battery module, which inherently limits the representation of full-scale battery fire scenarios. In realistic applications, fire propagation between adjacent modules or to nearby combustible materials is possible, and secondary ignition could significantly alter the HRR curve and fire dynamics. Moreover, scaling effects such as increased radiative feedback, cumulative heat release, and prolonged burning in multi-module configurations could lead to a more severe fire than observed in single modules.

Although the study is limited to single-module experiments under controlled laboratory conditions, it provides qualitative guidance and practical considerations for fire safety engineering and emergency response. The findings provide insights for ventilation, fire suppression, life-safety and structural protection design. The study did not measure battery gas release rate or room pressure; however room ventilation systems should be capable of managing substantial volumes of flammable gases below the lower flammability limits, and prevent deflagration. However, this is highly dependent on the gas release rate, which in turn depends on the battery design and failure scenario. The ventilation design should handle gradual gas accumulation and intermittent gas release, and mechanical exhaust ventilation is a possible approach. The room should handle sudden transient gas discharges and overpressure increases during TR events. The high variability in the max. peak HRR between tests has implications for fire safety design. Fire safety engineers should interpret these data results as a range of credible fire scenarios from similar ignition and boundary conditions rather than as a single deterministic HRR curve. However, it should be evaluated separately for other modules and conditions. The sharp HRR increased from 100 kW to 1000 kW within 30 to 60 s following ignition, indicating that the fire suppression system should be local and activated as early as possible to prevent TR propagation to adjacent modules; however, the efficiency of the fire suppression system requires assessment. Early gas detection should be designed to identify gas release during the preheating stage and the TR stage. Measured gas production rates (CO, CO2) depend on the cell's energy content as well as the number of cells undergoing TR prior to and after ignition (See Fig. 11). Although HF and other fluorinated compounds were not measured, literature indicates yields up to 20-200 mg/Wh of nominal battery energy capacity [58]. Their potential formation during cell venting must be considered for life-safety analysis. Measured heat fluxes reached peak values of more than 80 kW/m2 at a horizontal distance of 0.8m and more than 30 kW/m2 at a vertical distance of 1.4m from the module venting area (See Table 6). In practice, modules are installed in close parallel arrangements within a battery unit, and minimum separation distances between modules are very limited. The risk of fire spreading from module to module depends on the fire protective barriers between them. Thermal exposure data collected in this article can inform structural building protection requirements. Finally, the observed fire behaviour provides preliminary qualitative guidance for emergency response planning. Transition from gas release to flaming combustion is important for the training of fire service, as well as the need for protective measures against toxic gas exposure.

Recent research by Fire safety research institute (FSRI), part of UL research institute, has advanced the understanding of li-ion battery fire hazards through large-scale experiments and a standardized testing framework. The UL 9540A installation-level outdoor mock-up tests have characterized full-system behaviour, including jet flames, gas deflagration, and fire propagation between modules [23]. FSRI's residential-scale experiments, including e-scooters in enclosed bedrooms, demonstrated how accumulated off-gases and delayed ignition can lead to deflagration hazards within confined spaces [54]. In parallel, RISE has conducted important work focused on the fire behaviour of battery installations at sea [49]. Our results agree with these studies in showing a rapid gas release and the potential for sudden HRR escalation.

The present study complements these large-scale investigations by focusing on a single-module in a room, providing novel contributions: (1) detailed cell-level temperature measurements during module fire, (2) room-scale effects vs. open burning, as well as estimation of HRR using different calorimetry methods, and (3) a battery module with thermal barriers between cells. This work addresses the experimental variability, a common feature in fire experiments, arising from the chosen trigger method. Current standards do not prescribe a set of external heating scenarios or the characterization of non-flaming venting events and jet flames. This study also provides measurement locations for temperature and heat flux.

A notable difference from previously published large-scale studies is the battery design. Apart from cell chemistry and format, the modules tested here contained thermal insulation barriers between cells, which may explain the absence of large HRR pulses observed in some free-burning tests using unprotected battery modules [49,54]. It is also important to note that the HRR reported in FSRI and RISE studies was measured under a calorimetric hood, while the present work has the thermal effect of the compartment. Overall, this work is complementary to large-scale and standardized studies.

Thermal runaway (TR) is still an inherent problem today. The storage of high energy density li-ion batteries in confined rooms significantly increases the hazards of fire and/or explosion. Understanding the impact of a li-ion battery fire, which is able to propagate TR from cell-to-cell, is essential for designing robust fire safety measures in battery rooms, especially given that not all battery designs inherently prevent such propagation. This experimental study represents a relevant scenario in which TR propagates within a single module (NMC 6.6 kWh fully charged) but does not escalate from module to module.

Thermal exposure inside the room was characterised through measurements of gas temperature, heat flux, and gas measurements. These parameters were correlated with internal module temperatures, enabling a detailed combined analysis of fire development in the room and thermal runaway propagation.

The results revealed that simultaneous cells undergoing thermal runaway led to a rapid release of heat into the room within a short time frame. The fire development was characterised by a rapid-fire growth, intensified thermal feedback as sustained flaming and elevated gas release temperatures heated adjacent module cells, and multiple heat release rate peaks and jet flames. Three adjacent upper sensors in different thermocouple arrays recorded a simultaneous temperature increase exceeding 500 °C at gas ignition, for 30 to 60 s. The short temperature peaks at the upper TCs may be representative of the upper gas layer temperature; however, further investigation is required. Heat fluxes above 20 kW/m2 were measured both at the ceiling and on a target wall, which is close to ignition thresholds for many polymeric materials, including electrical cable insulation. The observed heat flux may have a limited thermal impact on structural elements (e.g. concrete or steel), but sustained heat exposure over a longer period could affect their thermomechanical properties.

It is recommended to estimate the heat release rate using both the oxygen consumption calorimetry (OCC) and the carbon dioxide generation (CDG) methods in battery fires, as their reliability depends on the fire development phase. OCC is recommended during phases with sustained flaming under well-ventilated conditions, particularly in fully developed and decay phases. CDG is recommended during phases of intermittent combustion or significant gas venting, such as following gas ignition and during the fire growth phase. The release of gases under the thermal runaway stage without flaming is detected as HRR. Where CDG may predict a HRR from the increase of relative CO2 and CO concentration associated with internal heat generation, and OCC predict a HRR from the reduction of relative O2 concentration. However, the physically meaningfulness of HRR during unignited TR still needs further investigation, but remains helpful in identifying the onset of the TR stage. After gas ignition, the variation of HRR peaks between methods is attributed to chemical phenomena during TR. The variation in HRR curves and the large differences in the first HRR peak value between experiments were primarily attributed to the number of cells undergoing TR simultaneously at gas ignition.

The applicability of temperature-time curves, such as ISO 834 and the hydrocarbon fire, for the assessment of fire resistance in structural elements is discussed qualitatively for small indoor battery rooms.

The findings underscore critical challenges for early fire detection and effective suppression in battery storage rooms, emphasising the importance of the gas venting rate from the battery, the gas layer accumulation in the room, time to gas ignition and compartment ventilation. The variability observed throughout repeated experiments also highlights the limitation of the chosen trigger method (a gas burner). The battery module design and the compartment design, such as ventilation, geometry and the configuration of the module, limit generalisation of the findings. Differences in heating rate, preheating duration, and the number of cells undergoing thermal runaway may contribute to the observed experimental variability, which needs further investigation. However, the data provide useful guidance for risk-informed design, development of fire safety measures, and validation of fire models and safety strategies for small indoor battery rooms.

The experiments provide valuable data for fire safety engineers, and it is recommended to consider design assumptions regarding:
•
Cell-to-cell TR propagation may occur within a module, and it is dependent on battery module design.
•
Consider delayed ignition and/or events without flaming, even if thermal runaway occurred. Ensure adequate dilution of flammable and toxic gases during gas venting.
•
The direction of jet flames is dictated by the ventilation opening of the battery.
•
Consider classifying the fire growth rate in the room with a growth constant k ranging from 0.5 to 1.0 min/√MW (normalized to 1 MW), which is faster than the reference ultra-fast t2 growth rate (k = 1.25 min/√MW).
•
The interpretation of fire development phases in battery rooms differs from compartment fire theory.
•
The fire spread, and the escalation within the room remains substantial when a single module burns.

Future research should emphasise testing different NMC variants, investigating fire spread between multi-module configurations, exploring ventilation effects further, and evaluating evaluating the effectiveness of active and passive fire protection measures in confined compartments.
